always.exam: Observation + palpation screen (lateral epicondyle, medial epicondyle, olecranon, cubital tunnel, radial head)
always.why: Baseline localisation of tenderness across the common elbow structures before targeted provocation testing.
always.confirmsOrExcludes: Screens which structure(s) are locally tender

always.exam: Active + passive elbow ROM (flexion, extension, pronation, supination) with end-feel
always.why: Range and end-feel is required to identify a capsular/OA restriction (extension loss earliest) versus a soft-tissue-limited presentation.
always.confirmsOrExcludes: Screens capsular restriction pattern (extension loss = earliest elbow OA sign)

always.exam: Elbow/wrist special test screen (Cozen's, Mill's, Golfer's elbow test)
always.why: First-line provocation cluster to broadly localise lateral (extensor) versus medial (flexor) tendinopathy.

add.exam: Cozen's test + Mill's test + resisted wrist extension (ECRB)
add.why: Lateral elbow pain is the classic lateral epicondylalgia (tennis elbow) location and warrants targeted confirmation.

add.exam: Cozen's test + grip strength comparison + load/technique review
add.why: A racquet-sport mechanism is a well-recognised lateral epicondylalgia risk factor and should be corroborated with provocation testing.

add.exam: Golfer's elbow test + resisted wrist flexion (FCR/FCU) + elbow valgus stress test
add.why: Medial elbow pain overlaps medial epicondylalgia, UCL sprain, and pronator teres syndrome — testing all three sources is required to differentiate.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes medial epicondylalgia, UCL sprain, pronator teres syndrome

add.exam: Golfer's elbow test + resisted wrist flexion with forearm supination
add.why: A golf-swing mechanism is a well-recognised medial epicondylalgia risk factor and should be corroborated with provocation testing.

add.exam: Elbow valgus stress test (+ milking manoeuvre) + throwing-phase history
add.why: A throwing mechanism creates repetitive valgus overload, the classic UCL sprain mechanism, and should be corroborated with valgus stress testing.

add.exam: Tinel's sign at cubital tunnel + elbow flexion provocation test + intrinsic hand muscle screen
add.why: Ulnar nerve distribution symptoms (little/ring finger) require confirmation of cubital tunnel entrapment and a check for advanced (intrinsic wasting) involvement.

add.exam: Tinel's sign at cubital tunnel + sustained elbow flexion provocation test
add.why: Symptom onset with sustained elbow flexion (e.g. phone use, sleeping) is the classic cubital tunnel aggravation pattern.

add.exam: Resisted supination + resisted middle finger extension + forearm extensor mass (mobile wad) palpation
add.why: Radial nerve distribution symptoms with lateral forearm pain distal to the epicondyle raise suspicion for radial tunnel syndrome, a lateral-epicondylalgia mimic.

add.exam: Resisted elbow flexion/supination (biceps) + cubital fossa palpation + observe for a Popeye deformity
add.why: Anterior/cubital fossa pain with a forceful eccentric loading mechanism raises suspicion for distal biceps tendinopathy or rupture.

add.exam: Olecranon palpation (fluctuance, warmth) + resisted elbow extension (triceps)
add.why: Posterior elbow pain over the olecranon requires differentiating bursitis (superficial, fluctuant) from triceps insertion tendinopathy.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms/excludes olecranon bursitis vs triceps tendinopathy

add.exam: Fracture screen (point bone tenderness, gross deformity, active extension check) — imaging referral if positive
add.why: Direct trauma to the elbow requires a fracture screen before any provocative special testing or loading.
add.confirmsOrExcludes: Screens for occult fracture requiring imaging referral before proceeding

supporting.exam: Neurological screen — C5-T1 dermatomes/myotomes/reflexes if referred/neurological concern
supporting.why: Rules out a cervical radiculopathy contribution when the pain pattern or neurological symptoms are atypical for a local elbow source.
Quantifies baseline elbow-related disability for goal-setting and re-assessment.

supporting.exam: Imaging (only if red flag, suspected fracture, failed conservative management, or mechanical locking)
supporting.why: Imaging is not routine for mechanical elbow pain; reserve for red flags, suspected fracture, or a structural diagnosis that will change management.
supporting.confirmsOrExcludes: Confirms structural pathology when clinically indicated
